name: Pricing Strategy
notes:
T-006 is done. Twelve sections, fully branded. Here's what's in it:
01 Executive Summary — all four tiers in one locked table with annual revenue per user
02 The Freemium Decision — the full rationale for why not paid-only, why not a time-based trial, and why the value-trigger paywall is the right architecture for this product and ICP
03 Willingness-to-Pay by ICP — WTP ranges for all four segments from the GTM, with the key insight that $19.99 sits at or above the WTP ceiling for Super Mom and Budget Family, which is exactly why the founding tier at $7.99 is the primary conversion vehicle
04 Competitive Landscape — six direct competitors with pricing and what they don't do, plus the argument that the real comparison isn't Mealime at $5.99 — it's the four apps she currently stitches together for $16–22/month
05 Plan Architecture — all four plan cards with full feature lists
06 Free Tier Design — every limitation explained with the reasoning behind it
07 ROI Framing — the payback period table (Founding plan pays back in under 26 days), with the $10/$111 framing flagged as pending approval
08 Founding Member Strategy — every parameter including the live counter mechanism and the scarcity framing rules
09 PLG Monetization — the three-milestone path to the paywall with all conversion targets
10 Paywall Recovery — the L-10 through L-13 sequence with each email's angle
11 Revenue Model — three launch scenarios (conservative / base / optimistic) with Month 1 MRR
12 Decision Log — every T-006 question answered and locked, with the one remaining open item ($10/$111 ROI framing) clearly flagged so T-006 can be marked complete

name: Customer Journey Mapping
notes:
This document is the complete operating architecture
for the easyChef Pro marketing and product system.
It serves three functions. First, wireframe reference:
every page, flow, traffic source, link, Firebase event,
and UTM parameter is defined here so every team
member builds from the same ground truth. Second,
job description foundation: each of the 15 roles in the
Reading Guide defines the sections that role owns,
the decisions they make, and the systems they
operate. Third, AI workflow source: every section is
structured as a deterministic specification so that AI
agents can execute campaign, content, and support
workflows without ambiguity. Nothing in the system
should be built, changed, or retired without a
corresponding update here.

name: Brand Guidelines & Visual Identity
notes: Logo, colour palette, typography, imagery style, icon system

name: Website Content (all pages)
notes:
T-011 — Website Content (All Pages) — Board Update
May 2, 2026
All website copy is complete and filed. Every page has been written, validated against the approved claims list, corrected for compliance, and delivered as a branded document ready for developer handoff. This task moved from not started to fully delivered in a single working session.
What was completed
Eight page documents are now filed under T-011. The original homepage document was reviewed and found to be four pages combined into one. It has been broken apart correctly and each section placed on the right page. The full site is now:
easychefpro.com/ — Homepage. Short directory page. Hero, three proof stats, four-stage overview, social proof, CTA. Links out to deeper pages.
easychefpro.com/experience — Features. Full TRACK / PLAN / OPTIMIZE / SHOP breakdown with the Yogurt Problem trust block and developer notes for screenshot placements and anchor links.
easychefpro.com/services — The problem page. 6:30 PM empathy story, fragmentation argument, Old Way / New Way comparison table, CTA. The page that converts skeptics.
easychefpro.com/story — Origin story. No names, warm and human. Credibility proof points and mission statement. Evergreen — live in Phase 1 from the Coming Soon footer.
easychefpro.com/pricing — Four-plan comparison (Free / $7.99 Founding / $19.99 Standard / $15.99 Annual). ROI table showing $1,240 net year-one saving on the Founding plan. Five FAQ pairs. 60-day guarantee band.
easychefpro.com/about — Brand and mission page. Three belief statements, full credential stack, no corporate registration details.
easychefpro.com/blog — Notes and Nibbles index page. Category filter, featured post card, three-card grid, email opt-in, and the complete six-pillar editorial plan with target keywords for the content team.
easychefpro.com/recipes — Full developer spec for the 10,000-recipe programmatic SEO engine. Template covers every page element including schema markup, image spec, UTM parameters, and Core Web Vitals notes. Dev builds once, it scales automatically.
Decisions locked this session
Founding price confirmed at $7.99 per month, 60% off the $19.99 standard rate, locked forever for the first 5,000 families. Free version confirmed at launch with no credit card required. Annual plan confirmed at $191.88 per year ($15.99 per month).
Product database updated to 800,000 products across all documents. Recipe database confirmed at 10,000 recipes at launch. Both figures are now consistent everywhere — website pages, GTM, homepage, and approved claims list.
Receipt scanning language is now store-agnostic across all assets — "scan any grocery receipt" — not tied to any specific retailer. Walmart sync (shopping list to cart integration) stays Walmart-specific until additional integrations go live.
All personal names removed from every document. Founder section on the homepage and story page is rewritten in warm, human brand voice with no ICS or command structure language.
The health goals percentage claim (previously 87.5%) has been removed from all assets and replaced with qualitative 6-dimension health scoring language. No validated figure exists yet — this will be collected from the beta program. The word "clinical" does not appear in any distributed asset pending legal review.
Compliance corrections applied
Every document was cross-referenced against the approved claims list before delivery. Corrections applied across the full document set: food waste percentage corrected to 69.5% throughout, patent language updated to 9 patent-pending technologies, "registered" added before dietitians in all instances, Instacart removed from all current integrations (flagged for reinstatement when live), founding discount corrected from 50% to 60% off everywhere.
What is still open
The $10 per month saves $111 per month ROI framing is written into the pricing page structure but flagged as pending Marketing Lead approval — the $1,336 annual figure is used in its place until that clears.
Developer to add anchor links on the experience page for each of the four stages. Beta family testimonials needed to replace the 2,400+ placeholder on the homepage at launch. Instacart line to be added back to the experience page when integration is live. The /pricing page requires an interactive ROI calculator — static fallback is in the document if dev resource is not available.
Timeline status
T-011 is complete on the content side with 37 days remaining before the June 8th deadline. T-012 (Website Pre-Launch Content) and T-017 (SEO) are now unblocked. All documents are filed and shared in the task.

1 Page Preview — warm cream background, 🎉 emoji first, "You're in." in large brand red, three confirmation bullets (inbox, July 1, price locked), share prompt with three buttons (WhatsApp / Facebook / Copy Link), quiet sign-off at the bottom. Celebratory, not transactional.

The red blocker callout at the top stays prominent — this page is the hard dependency for the entire A/B test. Nothing can run until it exists.

name: Content Strategy and Copy for the 404 Page
notes:
404
Tagline:
"This recipe doesn't exist yet. But your kitchen thinks it should."
It does three things at once: it acknowledges the error without apology, it personifies the kitchen (consistent with "Your kitchen, in command"), and it plants the seed that easyChef Pro is the system that fills gaps like this — turning "not found" into "not yet."
Supporting copy:
"Recipes disappear. Ingredients get forgotten. Dinner becomes a guessing game. That's not a recipe problem… It's a systems problem. easyChef Pro keeps your kitchen in command so nothing falls through the cracks."
This mirrors the brand's core truth directly on the error page, turning a frustration moment into a value proposition moment without feeling like a hard sell.
CTA (two-tier):
Primary: "Get early access" (waitlist)
Secondary: "Take me home" (back to homepage)
The primary CTA capitalizes on the micro-moment of need; the user was looking for a recipe, which means they're already in the target headspace. The secondary is a soft exit that respects their autonomy.

name: Customer Journey Map - Budget Family
notes:
T-010 Budget Family journey map — same brand guidelines format as T-009, with these key differences from the Super Mom map:
ICP validation gate is the first thing in the document — amber warning callout before any content, plus a full approval checklist in Appendix A5.
ROI claim flagged twice — at Stage 2 (acquisition) and Stage 5 (paywall) — with the $10/$111 framing quarantined until Marketing Lead approves it and the $1,336/year approved figure specified as the fallback.
Dollar-first framing throughout — every lifecycle email description, every stage callout, every retention note leads with the savings number rather than the emotional angle. The share mechanic at Stage 7 leads with their cumulative dollar total, not the app name.
Walmart integration called out as the primary retention driver for this ICP, not a secondary feature.
Active DL_ID registry included in Appendix A4 so Marketing Ops has a single reference for where this funnel sits in the live system.
flagNotes: CP 02 VALIDATION REQUIRED — This journey map is complete and ready to activate. Neither this document nor any funnel built from it may be distributed until Marketing Lead confirms ICP 02 is validated with real beta data. Status: PENDING.

name: Social/Email Campaign Strategies
notes:
Full strategy document — 8 parts covering everything:
Part 1 — The Universal Engine — the 7-step framework at both campaign arc level and individual post level, with a worked Facebook example showing all 7 steps
Part 2 — All 7 campaign types — Waitlist, Download, Founding, Referral, Affiliate, Recipe, Upgrade — each with exact CTA text, destination, and loss aversion angle
Part 3 — Social + Email Synchronisation — the full paired calendar showing social primes the day before email converts, with pair_id logic
Part 4 — Platform rules — Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, Pinterest, Nextdoor — dimensions, character limits, hook style, UTM codes, link placement
Part 5 — Pre-launch content calendar — Week 1 through Launch Day July 1 — weekly themes, platforms, post counts
Part 6 — Visual creative rules — image generation pipeline, brand overlay elements, claim bar auto-selection, gender enforcement
Part 7 — Brand voice — register per platform, approved phrases, forbidden phrases, tone litmus test
Part 8 — Measurement — Friday 5 metrics, attribution rules, weekly review process
File this alongside the Email + LP Workflow document. Together they are the complete campaign operating system.

The One Rule
Every campaign tells one story. That story has 7 chapters — Hook, Problem, Agitate, Solve, Value, Proof, CTA. Email and social are two voices telling the same chapter at the same time. Social speaks first. Email closes.

How They Work Together
Social posts prime the audience the day before the email lands in the inbox. When she sees the Facebook post about the $1,336 her family is wasting, she is already thinking about it when the email arrives the next morning. The email does not have to work as hard to get her attention — social already did that.
This is the pair system. Every email has a social partner. Same funnel stage. Same story theme. Same approved claims. Different channel, different format, same chapter.

The Campaign Arc — Both Channels Together
Day 0   📱 Social Post 1 — Hook       "Your family threw away $1,336"
Day 1   📧 Email SEQ-1-E1 — Hook      Subject: "Save $1,336 this year"

Day 2   📱 Social Post 2 — Problem    "It's 6:30 PM and the fridge is full but useless"
Day 3   📧 Email SEQ-1-E2 — Problem   Subject: "No more 6:30 PM dinner panic"

Day 5   📱 Social Post 3 — Agitate    "You've been duct-taping 5 apps together"
Day 6   📧 Email SEQ-2-E1 — Agitate   Subject: "Why nothing has fixed it yet"

Day 9   📱 Social Post 4 — Solve      "easyChef Pro closes the loop"
Day 10  📧 Email SEQ-2-E2 — Solve     Subject: "One app. One loop."

Day 13  📱 Social Post 5 — Value      "30 minutes. What you already have."
Day 14  📧 Email SEQ-2-E3 A/B — Value Subject: "Dinner stops being a crisis"

Day 24  📱 Social Post 6 — Proof      "Built by first responders"
Day 25  📧 Email SEQ-2-E4 — Proof     Subject: "Validated across 10,000 families"

+Day 28 📱 Social — Urgency           "Founding price ends soon"
+Day 29 📧 Email SEQ-3 — Urgency      Subject: "60% off ends when we hit 5,000"

July 1  📱 Social — Launch            "The app is live"
July 1  📧 Email SEQ-4 — Launch       Subject: "Early access is yours"

The Individual Post Rule
This same 7-step arc also lives inside every single post and every single email in compressed form. The campaign arc is the macro story. Each asset tells the micro version of the same story. So whether she reads one post or the full 8-week sequence she always moves through the same emotional journey — recognised, validated, agitated, shown the solution, convinced of the value, given proof, and asked to act.

Why It Works
She sees the social post and nods. She opens the email because the subject line mirrors exactly what she was just thinking. The landing page she clicks through to continues the same chapter. By the time she hits the CTA she has seen the same story told three times across three surfaces. The conversion feels inevitable — not like she was sold to, but like she made an obvious decision.
Every campaign type — Waitlist, Download, Founding, Referral, Affiliate, Recipe, Upgrade — runs this exact same engine. Only the final CTA destination changes.
